topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Symmetric functions
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Polynomial operations
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > QM-AM-GM-HM / Power Mean
prompt:
Given an integer number $n \ge 3$, determine the maximum value the product of $n$ non-negative real numbers $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ may achieve, subject to
$$
\frac{x_1}{1+x_1} + \frac{x_2}{1+x_2} + \dots + \frac{x_n}{1+x_n} = 1.
$$
response_text:
Let
\[
y_i=\frac{x_i}{1+x_i}\qquad (i=1,\dots,n).
\]
Then \(0\le y_i<1\), and the condition becomes
\[
y_1+y_2+\cdots+y_n=1.
\]
Also, solving for \(x_i\),
\[
x_i=\frac{y_i}{1-y_i}.
\]
Hence
\[
x_1x_2\cdots x_n=\prod_{i=1}^n \frac{y_i}{1-y_i}.
\]

So we must maximize
\[
\prod_{i=1}^n \frac{y_i}{1-y_i}
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
y_i\ge 0,\ \sum y_i=1.
\]

Take logs:
\[
\log\Bigl(\prod_{i=1}^n \frac{y_i}{1-y_i}\Bigr)
=\sum_{i=1}^n \left(\ln y_i-\ln(1-y_i)\right).
\]
Define
\[
f(t)=\ln\frac{t}{1-t},\qquad 0<t<1.
\]
Then
\[
f''(t)= -\frac1{t^2}+\frac1{(1-t)^2}
= \frac{2t-1}{t^2(1-t)^2}.
\]
This is not globally concave, so instead use Lagrange multipliers on
\[
\sum f(y_i),\qquad \sum y_i=1.
\]
We get
\[
f'(y_i)=\frac1{y_i}+\frac1{1-y_i}=\lambda
\]
for all \(i\), so all \(y_i\) are equal:
\[
y_1=\cdots=y_n=\frac1n.
\]
Thus
\[
x_i=\frac{1/n}{1-1/n}=\frac1{n-1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
x_1x_2\cdots x_n=\left(\frac1{n-1}\right)^n.
\]

It remains to justify this gives the maximum. Since the problem is symmetric and the objective tends to \(0\) when any \(y_i\to 0\), the maximum occurs in the interior, where the above critical point is the only one; hence it is the global maximum.

So the maximum product is
\[
\boxed{\frac1{(n-1)^n}}.
\]

Final answer: \(\displaystyle \frac{1}{(n-1)^n}\)
